html:
Lateral Motion Projection
⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
## Introduction

As objects move in the world, or as the camera moves, the projection of
the dynamic scene into the two-dimensional (2D) camera plane produces a
sequence of temporally varying pixel brightness. Before diving into how
to estimate motion from pixels, it is useful to understand the image
formation process. Studying how three-dimensional (3D) motion projects
into the camera will allow us to understand what the difference is
between a moving camera or a moving object and what types of constraints
one might be able to use to estimate motion.

## 3D Motion and Its 2D Projection {#d-motion-and-its-2d-projection}

A 3D point will follow a trajectory $\mathbf{P}(t) = (X(t),Y(t),Z(t))$,
in camera coordinates (@fig-optical_flow_basic_motion_point). As the
point moves, it has an instantaneous 3D velocity of
$\dot{\mathbf{P}} = (\dot{X}(t), \dot{Y}(t), \dot{Z}(t))$. The
projection of this point into the image plane location is
$\mathbf{p}(t) = (x(t),y(t))$ and its projection will move with the 2D
instantaneous velocity $\dot{\mathbf{p}} = (\dot{x}(t), \dot{y}(t))$,
where all the derivatives are done with respect to time, $t$.

![A 3D point, $\mathbf{P}$, moving in the world projects a 2D moving point, $\mathbf{p}$, into the camera plane.](figures/optical_flow/basic_motion_point.png){width="50%" #fig-optical_flow_basic_motion_point}

Using the equations of perspective projection $x=f X/Z$ and $y=f Y/Z$
(assuming that the camera is at the origin of the world-coordinate
system), we can derive the equations of how the instantaneous velocity
in the camera plane relates to the point motion in 3D world coordinates:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= f \frac{\dot{X} Z - \dot{Z} X}{Z^2} = \frac{f \dot{X} - \dot{Z} x}{Z}\\
\dot{y} &= f \frac{\dot{Y} Z - \dot{Z} Y}{Z^2} = \frac{f \dot{Y} - \dot{Z} y}{Z} 
\end{aligned}$$ The second expression is obtained by using $x=f \, X/Z$
and $y=f \, Y/Z$, which removes the dependency on the world coordinates
$X$ and $Y$. Note that $f$ is the focal length. In many derivations, the
notation is simplified by setting the focal length $f=1$. Here we will
keep it to make explicit which factors depend on the camera parameters
and which ones do not. We can write the last two equations in matrix
form as:

elements: origin O (camera center, small sphere)
image plane (translucent rectangular sheet at Z = -f)
optical axis (dashed line through origin along Z-axis)
3D point P (sphere)
projected 2D point p on the image plane (sphere)
projection ray from O through p to P (solid green line)
3D velocity vector dP/dt starting at P (thick red arrow)
2D projected velocity vector dp/dt starting at p on the image plane (thick red arrow)
predicted next-state projection ray from O to P + dP (dashed green line)
coordinate grid on the image plane to show 2D displacement clearly
text labels for O, P, p, dP (dot-P), and dp (dot-p)

interactions.label: Depth (Z)
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates that as a 3D point moves further away, its projected image velocity (optical flow) decreases due to the 1/Z division.

interactions.label: Lateral Position (X)
interactions.teaches: Positions the point laterally, showing how off-center points experience different radial motion components when moving along the Z-axis.

interactions.label: 3D Velocity X (X-dot)
interactions.teaches: Adjusts the horizontal velocity of the 3D point, producing direct horizontal flow in the image plane.

interactions.label: 3D Velocity Z (Z-dot)
interactions.teaches: Adjusts depth velocity. Pure Z-motion produces radial optical flow (expansion/contraction) centered around the optical axis.

interactions.label: Focal Length (f)
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates that a larger focal length acts as a zoom, scaling both the image position and the projected flow vector proportionally.

demo_steps.title: Lateral Motion Projection
demo_steps.narration: A 3D point P is moving parallel to the image plane with lateral velocity X-dot = 1.0. The projection ray maps both the point and its motion vector onto the image plane. Because the depth Z is constant, the projected velocity vector is a straightforward scaled-down version of the 3D velocity.

demo_steps.title: The Inverse-Depth Effect
demo_steps.narration: Watch what happens when we push the point P twice as far away to Z = 10.0 while keeping the 3D velocity identical. The projected velocity vector (dot-p) on the image plane shrinks significantly. This demonstrates why distant objects appear to move much slower than near objects—the 1/Z scaling of optical flow.

demo_steps.title: Looming and Radial Flow
demo_steps.narration: Now let's eliminate lateral velocity and make the point move directly along the camera's optical axis (Z-dot = -2.0, moving toward the camera). Notice that even though the 3D motion is purely longitudinal, it projects to a radial 2D velocity vector. This is the visual phenomenon of looming or expansion as an object approaches.

demo_steps.title: Focal Length Magnification
demo_steps.narration: Finally, double the camera's focal length f to 3.0. This pushes the image plane farther from the camera center. Notice how both the projected point p and its 2D velocity vector scale up. A longer focal length zooms in, magnifying both the static geometry and the optical flow.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Perspective view positioned slightly to the right, elevated, and looking back toward the camera center at the origin (e.g., eye: [6, 4, 8], target: [0, 0, -3]). This angle makes it easy to see the separation between the physical 3D elements and their flat projections on the image plane.
interactionPlan.notes: Ensure that the projection ray (green line) dynamically intersects the image plane at coordinates x = -f*X/Z and y = -f*Y/Z (using standard pinhole projection inversion if modeling the physical camera behind the pinhole, or matching the simplified diagram's virtual front-plane projection x = f*X/Z). Render the labels in 2D screen space aligned with the 3D points. Ensure the dashed green ray perfectly aligns with the tip of the dP/dt vector to show the mathematical projection of velocity.

Lateral Motion Projection
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: A coordinate grid is present on the image plane in the rendered figure but is absent in the original textbook figure.
The image plane in the original has a dark solid outline with no shading, whereas the generated figure has a semi-transparent blue-gray fill.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive recreation of perspective velocity projection with functional educational steps and highly accurate math.
action_items: Optionally add a toggle to hide the coordinate grid to match the clean original textbook style more closely.
Adjust the initial camera angle slightly to match the perspective angle of the image plane in the original figure.

feedback.rationale: The figure is highly accurate with an overall score of 4.8 and no failure modes, so further iteration on minor visual polish is unnecessary.
action_items: Optionally add a toggle to hide the coordinate grid to match the clean original textbook style more closely.
Adjust the initial camera angle slightly to match the perspective angle of the image plane in the original figure.
